examples.translation: The wedding will be on Saturday.

naturalMeaning: The same word covers both 'groom' and 'fiancé' before the wedding — context tells them apart.

examples.text: Жених ждёт у входа в загс.
examples.romaji: Zheníkh zhdyot u vkhóda v zaks.
examples.translation: The groom is waiting at the entrance to the ZAGS.

naturalMeaning: Like жених, невеста covers both 'bride' and 'fiancée' — the same word before and during the wedding.

examples.translation: The bride is choosing the bouquet.

naturalMeaning: Always used in the plural for the couple together — there's no everyday singular form.

examples.translation: The newlyweds are dancing their first dance.

naturalMeaning: Russian has no dedicated word for 'best man' — the groom's chief supporter, who also signs as the legal witness at the ZAGS, is simply свидетель, the same word used in court. See свидетельница for the bride's counterpart.

examples.text: Мой брат будет свидетелем на свадьбе.
examples.romaji: Moy brat búdet svidételem na svád'be.
examples.translation: My brother will be the witness (best man) at the wedding.

naturalMeaning: The bride's counterpart to свидетель — again, literally just 'witness', not a wedding-specific title.

examples.translation: My sister is the bride's maid of honor (witness).

literalMeaning: acronym for 'запись актов гражданского состояния' (registry of civil-status acts)
naturalMeaning: The government office where a marriage becomes legally official. This is the one appointment that counts on paper, whether or not the couple also has a religious венчание or a big банкет — see the culture notes.

examples.text: Мы расписываемся в загсе в пятницу.
examples.romaji: My raspísyvayemsya v zákse v pyátnitsu.
examples.translation: We're registering our marriage at the ZAGS on Friday.

pronunciationNotes: Final ж devoices to an 'sh' sound at the end of a word — a general Russian rule, not specific to this word.

examples.translation: He's now my husband.
She's now his wife.
There were a hundred guests at the wedding.
The bride's parents are welcoming the guests.

naturalMeaning: Guests shout this — often over and over — to make the newlyweds kiss and "sweeten" the wine. See the culture notes for the full logic of the ritual.

examples.translation: Bitter! Bitter! Bitter! (kiss to sweeten it!)

naturalMeaning: A wedding reception is structured around a long sequence of тосты, called out one after another by the тамада — see the tamada entry below.

examples.translation: The next toast — to the parents!
The guests are waiting for the newlyweds' kiss.
When everyone shouts "gorko", the newlyweds kiss.

naturalMeaning: A playful, elaborate ritual at the bride's door before the ceremony: the groom's party has to answer riddles, complete silly tasks, and pay small "ransoms" to the bride's friends and family before he's allowed to "win" her. See the culture notes for a full walkthrough.

examples.translation: The ransom of the bride starts at the entrance of the building.

examples.text: Жених должен выкупать невесту у подруг.
examples.romaji: Zheníkh dolzhén vykupát' nevéstu u padrúk.
examples.translation: The groom has to ransom the bride from her friends.

examples.romaji: Padrúgi nevésty pri gatóvili slózhnyye zagádki.
examples.translation: The bride's friends prepared difficult riddles.
Each task is harder than the last one.

naturalMeaning: Used half-jokingly during the выкуп невесты, where the groom's side literally haggles down the "price" demanded for the bride.

examples.translation: The best man haggles over every step (of the staircase).
The bride's side came up with the final challenge.

examples.text: Сторона жениха приехала с цветами и подарками.
examples.romaji: Staraná zhenikhá priyékhala s tsvetámi i padárkami.
examples.translation: The groom's side arrived with flowers and gifts.
Where are the rings?

naturalMeaning: In Russia, obручальные кольца are traditionally worn on the ring finger of the right hand, not the left — see the culture notes.

examples.translation: The wedding rings are lying on the little pillow.
The bride tosses the bouquet to her unmarried friends.
The newlyweds cut the wedding cake together.

naturalMeaning: The hired host who runs the whole reception: introducing toasts, calling on guests one by one, organizing games and contests, and keeping the multi-hour banquet moving. See the culture notes, and the toasts-focused pack for more on this role in action.

examples.translation: The toastmaster announces the next toast.
The bride's veil is very long.

examples.text: Она мерила свадебное платье три раза.
examples.romaji: Aná mérila svádebnaye plát'ye tri ráza.
examples.translation: She tried on the wedding dress three times.
The groom rented the suit.
A white limousine takes the newlyweds around.

examples.text: Медовый месяц они проведут в Сочи.
examples.romaji: Medóvyy mésyats aní pravedút v Sóchi.
examples.translation: They'll spend their honeymoon in Sochi.

examples.romaji: My razaslá li priglashéniya myésyats nazát.
examples.translation: We sent out the invitations a month ago.

examples.text: Церемония началась ровно в два часа.
examples.romaji: Tseremóniya nachalás' róvna v dva chasá.
examples.translation: The ceremony started at exactly two o'clock.

naturalMeaning: The actual legal moment — signing the register at the загс — is often just called роспись in everyday speech, as in 'у нас роспись в три' ('our registration is at three').

examples.translation: The registration only takes fifteen minutes.

naturalMeaning: A separate religious ceremony in an Orthodox church, distinct from the legal роспись at the загс — some couples do both, some only the загс, and венчание has no legal standing on its own. See the culture notes.

examples.translation: The church wedding was in a small church.
The reception lasted almost six hours.

naturalMeaning: A large round decorated loaf the parents present to the couple, who each bite off a piece without hands — whoever takes the bigger bite is jokingly said to be the head of the household.

examples.translation: The parents welcome the newlyweds with the ceremonial bread.
Everyone raised their glasses to the newlyweds.
Open the champagne for the toast!

translation: to marry / to get married (of a man, or of a couple together)
naturalMeaning: Used for a man marrying, and — in the plural — for a couple marrying each other: 'они женятся' ('they're getting married'). Compare выходить замуж, used only for a woman.

examples.translation: They're getting married in June.

literalMeaning: to go out behind a husband
naturalMeaning: The verb Russian uses specifically for a woman marrying; a man instead жени́тся. Learners often mix these up because English 'to marry' doesn't split by gender.

examples.text: Она выходит замуж в следующем месяце.
examples.romaji: Aná vykhódit zámush v slédushchem myésyatse.
examples.translation: She's getting married next month.

translation: to get married (as a couple, completed action)
naturalMeaning: The perfective partner of жениться, used for the couple together and emphasizing the completed event: 'мы поженились в прошлом году' ('we got married last year').

examples.translation: We got married last year.
We'll celebrate all night.

naturalMeaning: A slightly formal-sounding but very standard congratulation, often said right after the роспись at the загс, precisely because that signing is the moment the marriage becomes законный (lawful).

examples.translation: Congratulations on your legal marriage!

translation: "advice and love!" (a traditional wish for newlyweds)
naturalMeaning: An old, fixed toast-and-blessing formula wishing the couple harmony and mutual understanding (совет here means something closer to 'accord' than modern 'advice') alongside love — see the culture notes for more on this phrase.

examples.text: Совет да любовь вам на долгие годы!
examples.romaji: Savét da lyubóv' vam na dólgiye gódy!
examples.translation: Advice and love to you for many years!

examples.text: От всей души желаю вам счастья!
examples.romaji: At fsyey dushí zheláyu vam schást'ya!
examples.translation: From the bottom of my heart, I wish you happiness!
Live long and be happy!
